chunks.text: Good morning, Deborah. Welcome to the Education and Workforce Select Committee hearing for the Immigration Bill. We've got five minutes with you this morning, that includes time for questions, and we'll hand over to you now. Yes, good morning, Chair. Thank you. Nice to see you again and the committee again.

Well, I just thought that I would, as I did last week, just leave it open for questions. I think that's the best use of the few minutes that we have together. Wonderful. Yes, it is, especially given that we've got only five minutes. Do we have any questions from the committee? I've got one to start. I just wondered, we have heard a lot from all of our submitters really, the things that they obviously don't like about the bill or do like about the bill. What would you change?

We want to hear the constructive stuff. What would you change immediately? Yeah. The focus would be on removing the humanitarian appeals for visitor visa holders. I don't think there's a policy case for that. It's only going to affect just over 100 people a year, when over 400,000 visas are issued visitor visas. And, there's a high success rate for exceptional circumstances of a humanitarian nature that really affect families that are really doing it tough in New Zealand.

And it's just going to move that heat and stress in the system into ministerial appeals, to electorate offices, to the media. It's just going to create a lot of noise and disruption in the system, and my submission would say there's no justification for that. And one small follow-up question. Do you see that number escalating?

Because you said, it's only 100 a year. That's now. If it were to be left as is, do you see that number escalating? Those numbers have been pretty stable, and the reason there's been a small increase of a couple of dozen a month is because of the COVID lockdowns where people were here for years and had families and kids and that kind of thing. There's actually been a stable number, relatively, to the numbers. And when we're talking about this is the place, the specialist tribunal, that looks at our obligations concerning rights of the children in those international obligations.

Why would we move such complexity for such vulnerable cases in the community that create such disruption for the rest of the system? Why would we take it away from that specialist tribunal? Mm. Thank you. When there's been no business case for it. Yeah. Thank you. And a question from Phil Twy. Yeah. Thanks, Chair.

So Deborah, a couple of things. Drawing on your experience as a practitioner, could you talk about, and your knowledge of the data in terms of the appeals that go to the IPT for these humanitarian appeals against deportation. What are the kinds of cases? What are the kinds of people and the kind of situations that are in those numbers?

Yeah, sure. So first of all, this is a tough tribunal. And so there's about a 40% success rate, which means that's very high. And you've got cases like, and 40% of the cases are the Pasifika community. And they're cases like a family might have a child with autism or special needs or a disability, and they really need that auntie to stay because they've bonded with the kid, because they've been looking after the child.

Or the grandparents of there supporting that family with those children with those special needs. That seems to be a bit of a theme that I'm seeing coming through. Families with disability needs, needing those other families to support them. Yeah, that's quite common. Mm. And, Deborah, for the benefit of people who haven't had direct engagement with the tribunal, could you just talk a bit about how the tribunal works, the quality of its decisions, how it functions?

Yeah. So these appeals we're talking about, they're on the papers. So we're not talking about court hearings. The appellants have to pay quite a hefty fee, it's over $1,000 now, and that can always be increased if we wanted to manage costs. Appellants have to appeal within 42 days. It's quite a narrow window, which is probably why a lot of people don't appeal. And then it's a specialist tribunal.

It's internationally renowned. It's thorough, and it applies New Zealand's international obligations to these cases. So it's a place in New Zealand where we can have confidence that New Zealand is meeting our international obligations. So when we go to Geneva to report to the Human Rights Committee and so on, and the Committee for the Rights of Children and People with Disabilities, we know that that is a place in New Zealand that is looking after and guarding those obligations for us.

Mm. Could you, Katie, is that okay if I ask one more question? If it can be concise. I'm just trying to come off speech- Sure... phone to, yep. Thank you. Yeah. In these kinds of cases, Deborah, what are New Zealand's international obligations? Yeah, so they commonly involve the rights of the child, people with disabilities, discrimination against women, our fundamental human rights obligations in terms of even sometimes living conditions.

It's about exceptional circumstances of a humanitarian nature. And human beings can be complicated and messy, and this is a place where the tribunal can pick through all of the issues and consider whether or not there's an exceptional case on humanitarian grounds. And as I say, it takes heat out of the system, so we don't have front page media articles, electorate officers, ministers, having to grapple with these very tough cases.

Thank you so much, Deborah. Really appreciate your participation in this process and being available for us this morning. Thank you. And we'll move on now to our next submitter, who is Snow Ryan. Snow, hopefully we've got you online and ready.
There we go. Welcome. We have five minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions. Hopefully, we can see you. We can't hear you yet.
We definitely see you there. You just need to turn your camera on and unmute yourself.

There we go. Good morning. Ah, good morning, Snow. We have five minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for questions. So I'll hand it over to you now. We can't see you yet, but if you can turn your camera on, but we can definitely hear you. Just one moment. I'll try with the camera. I'm having problems with the Zoom meeting this morning, so.

Ah, that's okay. The point is that we can hear you, so it's not the end of the world if you wanted to carry on. Wonderful. I'll carry on so I don't waste any more of your time. Good morning, and thank you for the opportunity to speak this morning. My name is Snow Ryan. I'm an everyday Kiwi. I have submitted in writing on the specific provisions of this bill.

Today I want to use my five minutes differently, not to restate what is in my written submission, but to place a concern before the committee that I believe goes beyond the individual clauses. My written submission raises proportionality, oversight, and transparency. I stand by all of it.

But the deeper concern is this. The combined effect of these provisions creates a structural architecture that comparable democracies have demonstrated leads to serious harm. And through intent-- No, sorry, not through intent, but through the predictable logic of accumulated power without adequate constraint.

The United Kingdom did not set out to wrongly detain and deport British citizens. The Windrush scandal happened because of an accumulation of individually defensible choices. Extended liability periods, erosion of appeal rights, data sharing without purpose limits, administrative detention without judicial oversight.

The Windrush Independent Lessons Learned Review found that structural features, not individual bad actors, were the primary drivers of harm. Every one of those structural features is present in this bill. I have tabled a written addendum that sets out the case in a detail, which I have not yet forwarded to the committee, and I would do so under the instruction of the secretary.

But I want to name the most serious gap directly. This bill does not establish statutory flaws for immigration detention. No maximum period before judicial review, no prohibition on using prison facilities for people who have committed no crime.

No duty to consider alternatives before detaining someone. These are not technical omissions. They are the conditions that enabled enforcement practices in the United States and the United Kingdom that proved extraordinarily difficult to reverse once normalized.

New Zealand has a narrow window to build different foundations. That window is this committee and this bill. I'm asking for three specific amendments. Mandatory judicial review of detention within 72 hours, explicit purpose limitation preventing enforcement agencies from accessing data held by schools, health providers, and community organizations, and a statutory review period of no more than three years.

But I want to close with something larger. This bill asks Parliament to make consequential decisions about the kind of enforcement state New Zealand will become. Decisions that will shape experience of Pasifika communities, migrant workers, international students, and long-term residents for a generation.

Those decisions deserve more than a select committee process and a government majority. I'm an advocate for citizens assemblies. I believe this bill is precisely the kind of question a citizens assembly exists to answer. Not because this committee cannot do its job, but because the trade-offs between security, liberty, and the kind of country we want to be are not technical questions.

They are democratic ones. Ordinarily, New Zealanders, given time and evidence, are capable of navigating that complexity. They have not been asked. I urge the committee to consider recommending that the government convene a citizens assembly on immigration enforcement powers before the substantive provisions of this bill take effect.

That would not be a delay. It would be an act of democratic confidence. New Zealand has historically done this well. We can do it again. Thank you, and I open for questions. Thank you, Snow. It doesn't appear that we have any questions for you, but it does not mean there's a lack of interest at all. It means you've given us a really constructive and helpful submission. So we thank you for that.

Wonderful. Thank you. Thank you. We'll move on now to our next submitter, who we have New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties. For that, we have Stephen Enslin, and hopefully we see you online. Yes, there we are. All right. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions.

We'll hand to you now. Okay. Thank you very much. Good morning, everyone. The New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties strongly objects to this billMinister Stanford is quoted in Radio New Zealand article about the council submission saying that the ministry did not intend to limit people's freedom with this bill. "Did not intend" is about the best thing that the minister can say from our perspective.

First, it is admission that the bill does limit everyone's freedom, and second, it is an admission of error. The minister is telling this committee that the bill is broken and needs to be fixed. As we noted in our written submission, the Attorney General's Bill of Rights compliance report implies that the core functions of immigration compliance are so important that any limitation of our rights can be justified.

Mostly that's just another lazy report from the Attorney General, but it does accidentally introduce the topics which are at the core of this debate, which are: how important are the core functions of immigration compliance? And what problem is this bill addressing? The council believes that immigration compliance already have too many powers, not that they have too few.

If we cut immigration compliance inside our borders entirely, we would not merely be more free, we would be happier and more productive. Our immigration officers are already going around and harassing people, and more importantly from the council's perspective, interfering with our hard-fought liberties.

The council's main point is that our civil liberties are vastly more important than the core functions of immigration compliance. The second issue is why does this bill exist? The bill doesn't answer that question. It says things like improving operations or increasing effectiveness without the slightest awareness that those are not answers to the question. Why do operations need improving?

And more to the point, where is the evidence that the principal act is ineffective? The regulatory impact statement does not answer the question either. It merely says there is an opportunity to improve the effectiveness of the compliance powers. In short, the council does not believe that there is an effectiveness problem which needs addressing, and we think that the committee should agree with us that the minister has failed to present a compelling reason for change.

The council will not support any restrictive restrictions on the public's freedoms for the purpose of immigration compliance, except at the border. And the council remains steadfast in our belief that the existing power imbalance at the border, where people effectively lose almost all of our rights, is not sufficiently justified.

Civil liberties are under threat around the world. Immigration enforcement is being used overseas as a pretext for incarcerating people who have committed no offenses. Given this context, we should be amending the Immigration Act to prevent similar overreach from occurring here. This bill does the opposite.

We ask the committee to recommend that this bill does not proceed. I'll now open for questions. Thank you. We have a question from Grant McCallum. Yeah, got a couple here just quickly, Madam Chair. First of all, thank you for your presentation. I just want to establish first, do you think every country has the right to determine who can immigrate to it?

Who could immigrate to it? Yes. You do? Yes. Okay. And if people come here on a visa and their visa runs out, those people then should they leave? Or what should happen to them? Yes, those people should leave indeed. So- But enforcement powers could be given to police.

We don't need a specialty police force running around the country. Hold on. You think the police should go around and do this job? Not immigration officers who are professionally trained in this space and understand the law? I think that police are also trained and understand the law. Yeah, but immigration is quite a specific- The council believes. Yeah.

All right. Thank you. Okay. Thank you. And a question from Phil Twyford. Yeah, thanks, Chair. Stephen, thanks for your submission. Can you talk a bit more about why the evolution of immigration staff into being given increasing numbers of compliance and enforcement powers so that they start to behave like a police force, why you think that's a bad thing for rights and for democracy?

We note in our submission that the immigration claims not to be collecting evidence about the use of Section 280 powers. Mm. And so we stooped to repeating rumors, and the word that we hear is that immigration officers are out going to places of business, stopping everyone there who is a person of color, and demanding their papers already.

And we don't think that that is something we can support. Okay. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you, Stephen. We do appreciate your participation in the process, and your submission as well, and being here with us this morning. We'll move on- Thank you for your time and consideration. Thank you. We'll move on now to our next submitter.

We have Safe Haven Housing Trust Board. We have Brian Johnson appearing on their behalf. Brian, we have 10 minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions. Hopefully, Brian's jumping online. Brian, hopefully you heard all of that.

Just need you to turn your microphone off, turn your camera on, and we'll be with you.Brian, you are there. We can see your name on the screen. Yeah. Oh, there we go. We've got you. I had to unmute myself, yeah. For some strange reason, I wasn't muted, but then suddenly I was muted.

It's okay. It happens to the best of us. If we can have 10 minutes with you, and that includes time for questions, we'll hand over to you now. Yes. Thank you. I'm speaking to my revised submission that I sent in after I got legal advice to present the points that I wanted to make in a fashion that would suit the committee's analysis of the bill.

However, I would like to say that I've been involved, on an unpaid, voluntary basis, supporting asylum seekers since 1989. And up until 2018, I had worked with legal professional people and in my own capacity to promulgate 158 appeals of IPT decisions, and 124 of them were successful. I'm not saying I won them, but the support resulted in those people.

In perspective, I think in that time there were 3,500 cases that went through the IPT, so the number of appeals that were turned over is a very small percentage. But to the people whose lives were affected by winning the appeal, it was a hugely important and significant event in their life.

During that time, I discovered that Immigration never followed up the people who were deported. So a businessman in south Auckland funded me on several occasions to follow the people who were deported. You can understand that that put my own person at quite considerable risk. But that was how I learned the information about the fact that of the 34 that were declined, probably half of them should actually have also won as well, because the consequences of what they said was going to happen to them if they were deported from New Zealand actually happened.

And if any of the committee members want to ask me what some of those things were, I'm happy to answer. But I won't go into the detail. They are absolutely tragic and terrible circumstances. So we, as a trust, oppose this bill, and the trust broadly endorses five central concerns identified by the Law Society material, which is the removal of humanitarian appeal rights, expanded resident deportation liability, broader information and document powers, and the bar on further visa

applications after withdrawal of an asylum claim. And to that point, it is significant to say that the bill seems to be a reaction to the fact that of the 79 or 81 people we currently house who are waiting for an asylum claim, most of them have been with us for two years now, and they haven't even had their first interview at the RSU.

So if we then have penalties for crimes committed, well, I don't receive any government funding. I need a full-time psychologist. I asked in front of the board last Friday, "Of the people that we're helping, what percentage do you think have mental health problems as a result of the delays in the RSU processing?"

And to my astonishment, I was expecting her to say, "Oh, 20% or 25%." She said 93% of the applicants we have at the moment are showing mental health problems as a result of these delays. Now, when people have mental health problems, they often go and do things which they wouldn't normally do. So if that's a crime, then this bill seems to be saying that later on, they become a Kiwi, but they aren't a Kiwi, because once it's dug up that they had committed a crime, then they're deported. Well, the deportation of people who have won an asylum claim is a complete

farce concerning the asylum claim. So there should be no right of deportation of somebody who has had a successful asylum claim, is my opinion concerning this. The other thing is that the regulatory impact statement is actually untrue or incorrect in so many places in that statement that I would love to have 20 minutes of the committee's time to go through section by section of the RIS and show why that RIS is not reliable. And it astounds me that government members are being asked to make decisions based on an RIS that has

so many fallacies and so many errors in it. Concerning the withdrawal of asylum claims, over the years, I've known several of our clients who have withdrawn their asylum claims because they were high-level professional people. They came here.

Many over those 30 years, many of my clients were six years and eight years winning their appeal in the High CourtAnd in that time, they were discovered to have exceptional skills in commercial areas in New Zealand, and they were recruited by companies, and they gained a viable tax-paying job.

So then they wanted to withdraw or apply for residence on the grounds of their skills. So I don't believe there should be any limitation to that. That's an advantage to the New Zealand society to have a freedom of asylum-seeking people who have high-level skills or valuable skills to contribute to New Zealand, to be able to change their mind, particularly when we have two years you're waiting to get your first interview at the RSU.

In fact, I think if I research it, I'd find that it's even more than two years. So, the humanitarian appeal rights must be retained to take away the right for a judicial review or an appeal to the High Court of an asylum seeker who has not been found to be a successful asylum seeker by the IPT is no...

I have to say, over 37 years working with the IPT, I have the highest regard for the IPT, for its officers. They're fantastic people. But we're all humans, and if in 3,500 decisions, 158 could have gone a different way, that's not a reflection on incompetence or anything like that at the IPT.

It's just there are human factors, or there are evidential factors that the client couldn't produce that therefore made the IPT make the decision they made. So, the expanded deportation liability also worries me because over the years that I worked with so many people, I found that unfortunately some communities ring up Immigration and tell a whole lot of lies about the person to try to discredit their claim.

And this involves another added complexity for them. So I'm worried that if we give too much discretionary power to immigration officers without going through the due legal process, that some of those allegations made by people trying to cause harm to asylum seekers will be acted on, and the result will be that the person will be deported.

So the other concern I have comes from direct experience when, for two years, Elizabeth at the Asylum Seeker Support Trust and myself were given permission to go to the airport and interview arriving asylum seekers. And how they were treated at that time was just absolutely appalling.

I mean, I found one man one day chained by his arms and legs to the bed and was vomiting and was choking in his own vomit while he was being held in a room for three nights to be then deported from New Zealand without any due process to his claim. So I'm sorry, I can't support any interview basis at the airport that turns people around based on an interview. The other thing that astounds me is the fact that people are required to have evidence of their identity.

Over the years, I've known many people who have come into New Zealand to acclaim asylum who had no evidence of their identity and had to get that information sent to them because of the immediate departure they had from their home country. Sorry have I run out of time?

Sorry, that was just me sneezing. No, it's okay. Brian, you have got a very short time for questions. We have one question from Dr. Panjit Palmer if we-... can get that one through. Thank you so much, Brian, for your submission. I very quickly want to understand from you then what level of risk you think is acceptable for any kind of serious intervention to be justified.

Because you've talked of various methods where people are identified, and you don't like those ways. So just want to understand from you what level of risk you think is acceptable where interventions can be justified that are described in this bill. I don't think there's any justification. I think we have a robust present legal system that provides the opportunity for asylum seekers to claim asylum.

I do wish that in addition to what is done, that those people who are deported were tracked by Immigration, so they could, and the Justice Department, because IPT is part of the Justice Department, so they could do some self-analysis themselves on the quality of their decision-making.

But once again, I do not want to be seen to be saying that IPT is not competent. Most cases, I think that the IPT's decision failing somebody is usually based around the difficulty of them providing evidence of the claim they're making on account of the complete collapse of any civil and legal system in the country they came fromI remember recently, two years ago, a lady came to New Zealand from a country which I never really knew anything about.

But when I read their human rights record in the United Nations, I realized why would anybody ever want to live in that country? Because you just have no human rights. Therefore, very difficult for her to produce the evidence to support her claim. Thank you, Brian. We really appreciate you being part of this process in both your written and verbal submissions.

Could I just make one last statement, is that one of the difficulties I have is that our trust was only formed in 2019, and at that time, the last bulletin produced by Immigration at the airport was in 2019. I've been trying to get our trust listed in that bulletin so people arriving could contact us and receive our support. But the lady at the airport, Imogen Prickett, has told me that there is no funds to reprint the brochure. There is no funds to update the website. So therefore, people don't know that we exist. But we end up finding people living in the Manukau

Cemetery, the cemetery behind the church in the Mall, et cetera. So there's absolutely appalling things are going on, and something needs to be done to fund this. And we're not talking any large sum of money. Brian, might I recommend that we take that offline?

This isn't the process to deal with that concern, but it's a fair concern. So if you perhaps get in touch with the committee offline, we can direct you to the right person to get in touch about that. Thank you very much. Thanks again for being part of this process. Committee, we have a slight change in our schedule, and so we will be back for our next hearing at 9:50 AM. So if we can be back prior to that, a couple of minutes prior, that'd be great.Coffee

or tea, Vanessa? Coffee. Frothy? Yeah. Got your voice back, Vanessa? I have. I've got my voice back. Yay. Still a bit on the deep and guttural side- Right... but I'm not quite sounding like Mike Batterick- Lovely... like I was last week. That's funny.
Very good. I have to start saying, "Vanessa Weening," like I did Mike. Love it.

Very successful. Just organize a car vere for next season. Practical things carry on. Yes. My last year's one left, so I've got someone else. Important things carry on. Mm-hmm. I'm trying to work out how to manage my young worker. She's pregnant. She's due in November, so I've got to manage that.
Oh, cool. It'll be interesting in a physically demanding environment that is a dairy fest. Ali Sunder, Law. We've got Pooja Sunder and Stuart Dalziel joining us. Hopefully ready to go.
Here we go. No, there we don't go. That's Jenny. Here we go. Now we have.

Welcome, both. While you're getting yourselves sorted, we have 10 minutes with you. That includes time for questions. I think we've had you both on already, so you know what you're doing. Okay, time is yours. Go for it. Thank you. Morning. Yeah, well, we thought that we've put in our written submissions, so we probably suspect that you've read them, and therefore there wouldn't be much use in us reading them out to you. And we thought the time would be better used for any questions that you have for us based on our submissions.

Thank you for that, both of you. That is helpful because we do quite often have everyone recite their written submission for us a second time. Look, we have a question already from Ricardo Menendez March, so we'll start with Ricardo. Thank you very much, both. Hey, just in layman terms, what are your concerns for particularly children that could come about due to the changes to humanitarian appeals?

Stuart, you want to take this one? Yeah, well, the tribunal would make a written decision where they would outline specifically how they took into account the welfare and best interests of the child as a primary consideration. Whereas you remove that and you put that as an absolute discretion to a minister or a delegated decision-maker who needs to provide no reasons whatsoever, then the question becomes is, well, have they really considered the welfare and best interests of the child, and in what way?

Because I think of it in terms of one of the suggestions as, well, people can make a request to Immigration New Zealand to cancel their deportation liability under Section 177. And under Section 177, immigration compliance officers have a duty to consider international obligations.

But of the nearly 1,000 DLNs issued in the past five years, compliance has canceled 15 of those. Yet when people have made humanitarian appeals, 50% of those people who have appealed have won. So what that tells me is, is there's something deeply wrong with the process that we have at the moment, that immigration officers and compliance are not really considering the international obligations because if they were, we would be seeing a far higher level of cancellations than we see at the moment. And those decisions, again, are absolute discretion,

so therefore you don't know what's actually happened. And sorry, just for the committee briefly, what are some of the obligations that you're concerned that are being ignored? Some of the, like you don't have to name them. Well, the welfare and best interests of the child, family unity, disability rights.

The list goes on and on. There is a vast array of international obligations that New Zealand has signed itself up to, but it would appear that when you look at the stats, that compliance is failing New Zealand and those obligations because the story that's told on the tribunal decisions says something different.

And just to be very, very clear, we're talking about 0.13% of all visitor visa holders who've made this appeal. Made this appeal, not been granted the appeal, made the appeal. So very, very, very small numbers. This is being blown out of proportion.

Okay, thank you. We have a question from Phil Twyford. Yeah, supplementary on the same thing, really. Pooja and Stuart, you're both very experienced at dealing with cases that go to the IPT. What's your view about the quality of their work and the rigor, and how do you rate them as part of the sort of institutional framework of immigration policy?

I think there's a reason they were created to begin with, that we definitely needed this, and we definitely needed a judicial body to consider the humanitarian circumstances, consider the international obligations, and ensure that this test was being done in a way that actually gave proper merit to our international obligations. Look, there will be cases that we will appeal from the tribunal.

There'll be cases that we win at the tribunal. There'll be cases that we lose at the tribunal. And I think every person and every body at every point has the opportunity and should really improve. However, I think if we compare them to decision-making by an absolute discretion decision-maker, it's night and day. Mm. The basics is that at least we can see why they've made that decision and the reasons behind it, whereas we're never going to be able to see that from another decision-maker where it's absolute discretion.

Hmm. Yeah. And what about, I don't know if you've got the numbers at your fingertips, but what about how rigorous and demanding the APT is in terms of, and the numbers of, in terms of winning and losing cases, appeals? I think it was about 50% that we had a look at, and Stuart did crunch the numbers.

And the thing is though, 50% is such an incredibly low number overall, right? We're talking about half a million visitor visas, and we're talking, oh, half, and then .13 of them actually make an appeal. And then half of them succeed. So excuse my maths, but we're looking at about.07 of all-... visitor visa holders who make these appeals. And here's the thing, the tribunal holds an incredibly high standard.

This is not that everyone who lodges that appeal is getting it. The Supreme Court and the Court of Appeal have also outlined how high that standard is. Exceptional humanitarian circumstances is exceptional. It is not special. It's not just, "Oh my God, I feel bad for this person." It is exceptional. And then there's a second one, whether it's unjust or unduly harsh. You might find it exceptional and say, "Yep, no, but unjust or unduly ...

Not unjust or unduly harsh for them to be deported." Might find both and still say, "You know what? Still, this person has offended," or, "This person has done something that makes us feel-"... that they're not in the public interest to let them remain in New Zealand. So there are three separate stages to go through. It's not an easy appeal. So chair, got time for one more sub? Yep. One more.

So, for committee members who haven't dealt with these cases, actually, what does exceptional circumstances mean? Can you give us some examples so people can kind of imagine what- Yeah... are we talking about? Oh, so many. One, potentially if, for example, a parent is in New Zealand and something's happened in their home country, a coup or something along those lines, where they cannot go back, their house has been taken, the people they were living with have been arrested for whatever reason because of a coup in that country. They cannot physically go back.

There is nowhere to go back. That's one. Mm. Another one could be a New Zealand citizen partner who can't support partner number three because they've faced family violence and supported someone else in the past. Now they've got a child who is incredibly attached to partner number three. They're very close. There is psychological evidence to show that the child will suffer real developmental concerns, New Zealand citizen child, real developmental concerns if partner number three, the unlawful person, has to go back now.

That is what exceptional humanitarian circumstances are. It's not, "I've overstayed here. I've been here for 10 years. Come on, give me a visa." Mm. Tribunal routinely- Or, you know, "Come on, a visitor visa. Give me a visa." The tribunal routinely says just being in a relationship with a New Zealander is not exceptional in and of itself. There needs to be something else that's going to take it over the line.

So, I wouldn't be led to believe that the tribunal's an easy touch, and therefore we need to take it away from them and realign things. If the consideration was that the tribunal's an easy touch, then change the test that the tribunal has to administer.

At least then we have some clarity of what it is. I think the thing, as Puji said, we looked at the numbers. It seems to me that this bill is driven by I don't know what, because the stats don't actually suggest that we have a problem. The minister talked about, I think there was 14 people who've claimed refugee status who have committed crimes, and therefore we need to tighten the screws around the refugee.

That represents 0.7% of claimants. Again, it's the wood chippings on the floor that we're brushing up here to- Yes... in order to make up a bill. It just seems unnecessary. There are other things that everyone can focus on that are more important.

Thank you. Thank you very much, both of you. That is the time that we've got for you this morning, but appreciate, again, your written and your, obviously the chance we could have to ask you some questions as well. So that's really appreciated. And we'll move on now to our next submitter. Thank you. Now, hopefully, during that time, we've been able to get Wellington Community Justice Project online, Max Irving.

We have the Asia Pacific Refugee Rights Network. Ah, wonderful. Yeah, look, let's just be agile, and we'll welcome Klaus and Hafsa on for the Asia Pacific Refugee Rights Network. We have 10 minutes with you this morning. Welcome. Do we have Klaus coming on as well?

Klaus will not be joining. Ah, that's okay. So I'll- Well, it's all with you. That is okay... I'll welcome- Welcome to the committee... it will be only me. Okay. We have- Thank you. Speaking on behalf of both of us. Brilliant. We have 10 minutes with you, so that includes time for questions, and we'll hand over to you now Sure. Thank you so much. Tena koutou katoa. Thank you, Chair and members of the committee for the opportunity to speak today. So my name is Hafsa Khamisuddin, and I am the Co-Secretary General of the Asia Pacific Refugee Rights Network,

which is also known as APRRN. APRRN is a regional network of more than 300 civil society organizations and individuals across 28 countries in Asia and Pacific region, including New Zealand. Our members work to advance the rights of refugees, asylum seekers, stateless people, internally displaced people, and other people on the move.

I also speak today from my own lived experience. I am a former Rohingya refugee from Myanmar. I came to New Zealand in 2019 and became a New Zealand citizen only last year. So I speak not only from a regional advocacy perspective, but also as someone who understand personally what it means to seek safety and rebuild life in a new country.

I want to begin by acknowledging New Zealand's longstanding role in refugee protection and resettlement. New Zealand has provided safety and a new beginning to many people from our region. Its Refugee Quota Program, Community Sponsorship Initiative, and humanitarian commitments have made a real difference in many people lives.

That is why APRRN is deeply concerned about the Immigration Bill and the related parliamentary paper. Our central message is simple. APRRN urges the committee to recommend that this amendment be withdrawn or, at the very least, substantially reconsidered.

We are concerned that the proposed changes risk undermining New Zealand's reputation for fairness, human rights, and refugee protection. They may also have serious consequences for refugees, asylum seekers, migrants, children, women, and other people who are already in vulnerable situations. So I would like to focus on three main concerns and then briefly address any wider impacts. The first concern, APRRN is concerned that the proposed power for immigration officers to request identification based on suspicion, even if the intention is administrative, this power

may have discriminatory impacts in practice. People who are visibly migrants and from refugee background, Muslim, Asian, African, Middle Eastern, or from the racialized communities may feel they are being treated as a suspicion because of how they look, how they speak, their accents, their name, ethnicity, or country of origin.

For refugees and asylum seekers, this can particularly be harmful. Many have already experienced state surveillance, arbitrary questioning, police harassment, or threats from authorities in their countries of origin. When similar dynamic appear in a country of supposed protection, it can trigger fear, trauma, and mistrust.

The practical effect may be that people avoid public institution. They may avoid hospital, police, schools, employment services or family violence support because they fear immigration consequences if they do so. This does not make communities safer. It pushes people further into the shadows.

Secondly, APRRN is deeply concerned about the proposed restriction on alternative visa pathways for people who withdraw asylum claims. This one is the most troubling parts of the proposal. People may withdraw asylum claims for many legitimate reasons. Their circumstances may change. They may enter a genuine relationship with New Zealand partner.

They may marry. They may have children. They may receive a job offer. They may become eligible for another visa pathway that better reflects their current situation. A rigid ban on alternative visa pathway risks punishing people for ordinary legitimate life change. For example, if a person seeking asylum forms a genuine and settled relationship, it seems unnecessarily harsh to prevent them from applying for a partnership visa simply because they previously sought asylum.

Similarly, if someone has skill, employment, and opportunity to contribute to New Zealand, the law should not automatically block that person from regularizing their status. Thirdly, APRRN is concerned about the proposed limitation on humanitarian appeal rights. Humanitarian appeal rights are not a loophole. They are important safe ground. They allow independent oversight in complex cases where removal of New Zealand may have serious consequences.

This safeguard is especially important for people with trauma history, health concern, family responsibility, or risks of harms if they return. Limiting appeal rights may have serious consequences for children. Many families facing deportation have children who have grown up here, attend school here, speak English, have friends here, and may have very limited connection to their parents' country of origin.

Deportation can disrupt education, damage mental health, separate families, send children to unfamiliar environments where they may not speak the language or have support. New Zealand has obligation under the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. Meaningful humanitarian appeal rights help ensure that children's best interests are properly considered before life-changing decisions are made. I also want to briefly highlight the wider impacts of this bill.

For refugees, safety is not a physical safety. Safety also means stability, dignity, belonging, and the ability to rebuild their life without constant fear. Many refugees arrive here after years of persecution, insecurity, displacement, detention, exploitation, or family separationMeasures that increase surveillance, reduce appeal rights, or create fear of deportation can worsen anxiety, depression, trauma responses, and insecurity.

From my own experience, even with English language skill that I had when I first came here, work experience, and some understanding of institution, my first years in New Zealand were not easy. It took time to understand system, become independent, and feel grounded. For people facing language barrier, trauma, family separation, racism, poverty, disability, and insecure immigration status, the challenges are even greater.

The law should support people to feel safe enough to participate, contribute, and belong. It should not deepen fear. We're also concerned that this bill may contribute to negative public discourses about refugees and migrants. Around the world, refugees and migrants are increasingly used as political scapegoats. They are blamed for crime, housing pressure, economy difficulty, public service strain, or social decline. New Zealand has historically taken a more principled and balanced approach. Our concern is that the bill may unintentionally shift the

public conversation in a more punitive and suspicious direction. That would be harmful not only for refugees and migrants, but for New Zealand society as a whole. Finally, APRN is particularly concerned about the impact on women and girls and other groups facing heightened vulnerability. Women and girls with insecure immigration status may already face family violence, gender-based violence, sexual exploitation, trafficking, workplace exploitation, economic dependence, and language barrier and social isolation.

If immigration settings create more fear of enforcement and deportation, women may be less likely to seek help. A woman experiencing violence may decide not to call police, not to access healthcare, or not to report abuse, and not to approach a supportive organization because she fears immigration consequences for herself and for her family. The restriction on alternative visa pathways may also have gender impact.

Some women may withdraw asylum claims because of family pressure, trauma, misinformation, dependence on a partner, lack of legal advice, or relationship changes. A rigid ban could leave them with fewer options to regularize or escape unsafe situation. We therefore ask the committee to assess these reforms through a gender, child rights, disability, race equity, refugee protection, and trauma-informed lens.

In conclusion, APRN respectfully urges the committee to reconsider the proposed amendments. New Zealand should not move away from its profound tradition of refugee protection, fairness, human rights, and humanitarian leadership. At a time of rising displacement, shrinking resettlement places, reduced humanitarian funding, and increasing anti-migrants rhetoric globally, New Zealand leadership matters now more than ever.

APRN recommends that the committee withdraw or sustain consideration for the proposed amendment bill for that matter. And thank you again for the opportunity to speak. I welcome your question. Thank you. We have time for one very quick question from Riccardo Mini Desmarch.

Thank you, and I hope you can hear me okay. Yeah, great. My quick question is, what do you think will be the impact on the wellbeing of asylum seekers under the proposal of this bill? If you wouldn't mind, just as someone who's experienced the system and works with people experiencing the system. Well, I have also briefly mentioned in my statement, I will re-emphasize it again, because for the asylum seeker, this may be the one last hope in their life, right?

So they have a lot of oppression and surveillance in their own country, like myself, and maybe somebody was born as a stateless person like myself. So coming to another country, seeking this protection may be their one last hope for their survival, for their family, for their future. So having this immigration amendment and on this enhancement is like constraining for their every possibility to exercise their fair human rights under the refugee protection law. It can mean physical, it can mean mental, and not only for that person alone, also for the person

family, whanau, community as well. Because when somebody is dealing with mental health and hopelessness, it will inevitably also impact their family and their communities as well. And the worst-case scenario will be, well, I can relate to myself. When somebody finding a position there is no reason to live for, there is no hope to look forward to, it can be somebody thinking even taking their own life.

I had that thought for myself a couple of times when I was still seeking protection in country of refuge. Did that answer your question? Thank you very much for being part of this process. I do appreciate it, and for your written submission as well. So, we'll move on now to our next submitter, but thank you very much, Afsar, for joining us.
Now we have Matthew Robson, who is joining us. Thank you for being agile, Matthew.

There we go. You'll have to just unmute and turn your camera on. We will be able to see you and hear. There we go, and we have five minutes with you this morning, Matthew. That includes time for questions, and we'll move on to you now Thank you very much. I would've liked to appear in person.

It's always better, but I understand the restrictions you've got this week, having been there. When I was in Cabinet in 2002, Helen Clark said to a minister who brought a bill before the Cabinet that was particularly repressive, that the department of that minister had been waiting for a minister like him to try and push this through the Cabinet. That is what's happening with this bill, in my submission.

There is no problem with overstayers. There is a problem, root, stock, and branch, with the Immigration Act. That's what I'm appealing to members of this committee to do research on this question. And there are many of us who are lawyers, members of Parliament, who know the problems that people who are migrants and their New Zealand families face with the strictures of immigration.

What you're looking at is the foot of the elephant. Every day that members of Parliament have their cornflakes in Copperfield's and put kiwi fruit on it, it's possibly picked by somebody who doesn't have a visa. Now, this minister that Helen Clark berated with her famous death stare withdrew the particular bill. I'd suggest that Erica Stanford do the same because we've got this three-years cycle when a particular issue gets beaten up as a moral panic, that there are overstayers causing a problem.

The problem, as members of Parliament who are honest, and I'm saying they're all honest, all members of Parliament are honest, is that when someone presents with a problem with Immigration, they've felt that they've been dealt fairly with by Immigration. I could wallpaper my house with rejection notices, deportation notices over a period of 30 to 40 years dealing as a lawyer, a member of Parliament, and a minister on this question.

And I found that the person who's become illegal, who is classed as an overstayer, that the problem can go back to Immigration, to underpaid, overworked, untrained people often making decisions which are administrative law decisions, which means you can't get at the person.

We also, I would suggest, have a big problem, which I'd ask this committee to have a look at, at what happens to migrants or intended migrants at the border. I've spent countless days trying to get through and act as a legal representative, and my colleagues the same, at the border to have people turned away, not questioned in their own language, not given the right to talk to a lawyer, and shanghaied on a plane back from New Zealand to where they came from without their case genuinely being looked at.

I also, in my submission, and I take it from the clerk who was very helpful, that all members have read the submissions diligently, and of course they do. All members of Parliament on select committees go there with an open mind and not what is dictated by their whips, which is a really good thing about our system, to look at the Zawi case if you want to look at what the problem really is. And the problem will be the denial of justice and of what is now, in my opinion, the usual bidding war on who can be tough on withdrawing rights from immigrants.

So I'm going to just return, I only know I've got a couple more minutes left on this committee, but to the Zawi case, because I think it's instructive to go back to that. It's 2002 to 2007, at the Parliamentary Library, if your own research teams want to give it to you, will give you the Refugee Status Appeals Authority decision on this man.

Why it's important? Because he was classed as a terrorist. He was kept in prison for two years. He was on restrictive bail for two years. And then in 2007, the new inspector general found that his case was a load of cobblers, that Immigration, Security Intelligence Service, and unfortunately my colleagues, the Prime Minister, the Attorney General, the Minister of Immigration, had conspired to remove all his rights of appeal.

And that's what happens with people when you take away their appeals. They don't get justice. I've been many times berated by compliance officers who I have a love-hate relationship with, with Immigration, because my job is to act as it does for members of Parliament when people come to them for help, for the person putting their case to have their case heard.

And I've been told, "You're undermining the integrity of the immigration law." Because these compliance officers on the front line, I feel for them sometimes, they know that lawyers, and all of my colleagues, employers, and some members of Parliament, of course, are helping people to stay in a job somewhere, in a house well away from the authorities while they find out the facts.

They're not trying to dispute the law. They're trying to uphold the law, because justice is also part of integrity. Fairness-... is part of the law. Thank you, Peter. Yes, unfortunately we have reached the end of your five minutes. Oh, it is unfortunately. I'm sure you'd like to hear me all day on this topic. We do appreciate you being part of this process, and thank you for your written submission as well.We'll move on now to our next submitter. We have Asylum Seeker Support Trust, Dawit Arshad and Becky Little.
Hopefully,

joining us. There we go. We have Becky. And sorry, hopefully, I'm pronouncing Dawit correctly. Oh, we've got you together, hopefully. Yeah. There we go. Beautiful. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for questions. So we'll hand over to you now.

Hello. Tena koutou katoa. We are Dawit and Becky of the Asylum Seeker Support Trust. Thank you for inviting us to come today. We are a charity supporting over 3,000 people from all over the world, seeking asylum and safety in New Zealand from persecution, war, conflict, and climate change.

So we are uniquely placed to speak particularly to the human impact of the proposals in this bill. To be clear, we oppose the overall approach of this bill and the parliamentary papers relating to people seeking asylum and refugee. We won't go into full details of our written submission, but we would like to highlight a few of our main concerns today.

As someone who arrived here under the quota refugee programs and has called this place home for 28 years, carrying both the weight of where I came from and the gratitude for where I have been able to rebuild my life, and I say this, I'm most deeply upset by the new powers proposed for immigration officers, requested ID on suspicion of visa, a breach of being liable for deportation.

In practice, regardless of a person's status, lawful or not, temporary residence or citizen, it's clear they will be used disproportionately against people based on their race, ethnicity or country of origin. It will be particularly harmful to people seeking asylums and refugee. Many have fled countries where question document check and government monitoring were used to intimidate or control peoples.

Aotearoa should be a place of safety, not fear and suspicion. Becky, please. Likewise, the ban on alternative visa pathways for people who withdraw their asylum claim at Clause 11 is cruel and counterproductive. It is cruel to ban people seeking asylum from getting a partnership visa because they have fallen in love and are in a settled relationship with a New Zealand partner. It is cruel to ban people seeking asylum from an alternative visa because they have been offered a job that New Zealand needs filled, like the person in our hostel who because of their

skills was offered a job in our public sector. Everybody wins in this scenario. If a person finds certainty and safety they are seeking in an alternative way, it is cruel to keep them locked into an often traumatic asylum process. And the actual reality for most people and families seeking asylum in New Zealand is that it is a long and certain and hard life.

And to be clear, there are many things in this bill that we find problematic and oppose. The proposals in the parliamentary paper relating to kicking out claims for administrative matters such as missed biometric tests or limiting their rights of protection because of notions of bad faith, whatever that means, don't hold up legally in terms of the law and are not justified on the numbers or evidence. And we refer you-- We can answer questions about that if you'd like. What we'd like to focus on today is that in justifying this bill, there

has been a characterization of people seeking asylum that the vast majority are without merit. This is not true, and we wanted to take you through the facts in terms of the numbers and how asylum claims actually play out. In New Zealand, we have a very small cohort of people seeking asylum, representing a tiny proportion of our population, irrespective of the recent increases. And given the current global perspective and picture, this increase should not be the shock or surprise that it has been portrayed. Currently, 170 million people are forcibly

displaced, and this has doubled in the last decade. We have a tiny and minute 0.04% of our population that seeks asylum here every year. And for the last 10 years, at first instance, irrespective of the number of asylum claims per year, 25% to 35% of asylum claims are successful, but 40% of those declined are overturned, which brings up the amount of asylum claims that are ultimately successful to around over half. And those claims remaining declined after appeal, some of these will be successful on a later asylum claim because they find more

evidence to support their claim, or they may have real reasons to feel unsafe or persecuted in their home country and seek asylum abroad, and they are found to have credible harm but did not meet the legal threshold required for protection and recognition as a refugee. And when we looked at 50 of the last appeal decisions in relation to those that were dismissed, 74% of these had credible harm, but they did not meet that high legal threshold.

So on these numbers, a big pillar for the justification of this bill and associated paper is that the vast majority of asylum claims are without merit. It's simply knocked away. In reality, the current backlog of claims is a consequence of a failure to resource the system properly in response to a complex global picture and our own economic policies of trade agreements, including visa pathways with countries that have problematic human rights records, meaning that some migrants will seek refugee or protected status at some stage.

This is not a problem with the law or the processes. This is system working as intended. However, rather than explain this whole complex picture contributing to the pressure and resourcing it appropriately, responsibility for poor planning is effectively laid at the door of people seeking asylum themselves. With this bill being a vehicle for doing just that. It is a poor way to make law, and in doing so, employs a damaging narrative about people seeking asylum that is not accurate, supported, or justified and is reckless to the safety of a small and vulnerable community.

Instead, let's talk about resourcing the asylum system properly. Let's talk about greater help with support to restore mental health, wellbeing, integration for people who seek safety who are living a life of extreme uncertainty. We oppose this bill and paper, and we recommend it not to be put forward for second reading. Thank you. Thank you both. We do have time for a question, hopefully maybe two. One from Phil Twyford first.

Thanks you two for that excellent submission. One of the issues that we haven't heard much about or talked much about so far on the committee is the provision that would allow Immigration New Zealand to decline an asylum claim based on the failure to engage with biometric testing. Can you just explain for the committee what is this about? What is the biometric testing?

Have you been able to glean any evidence or data about this perceived problem that would justify giving Immigration this power? No. There is no evidence really to support that this is a massive problem for Immigration. This is also a particular administrative measure that is moved within the refugee process. At the moment, there's like three different groups go at having a biometric test at different times.

And what we know and what we see from what we do, and we were talking about this in the office, is that people missing their biometric test must be incredibly small because what we see is people being very desperate to kind of get to these tests because their asylum claim is their number one priority.

They're also trying to live in an uncertain environment, but their asylum claim is their number one priority. And it used to happen when you went for your interview, and you were surrounded with your lawyer, you had an interpreter, so it was less intimidating. Mm-hmm. And now they've brought that forward to before you get your confirmation of claim letter, you're required to go.

And then there's this second group that is like the sweep-up group that they're trying to find. Yeah. And really this is all about kicking claims out earlier. It doesn't seem to be supported by any evidence that there's any kind of nefarious reasons for missing your biometric test. It's not what we see. Thank you.

Thank you. And our next question from Ricardo Menendez March. Yeah. Would you mind expanding on, I guess, what do you see could be the impact on asylum seekers' well-being, engagement in public services, and the consequences to their mental health in relationship to some of the provisions of this bill?

And actually just start impacting the communities, not only asylum seekers, the refugee and the Kochari refugees and within the communities. Mm-hmm. So this is totally already starting. Not only them, even I myself personally impacted. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you both. Thank you for being part of this process this morning and for your written submission as well. It is really helpful for our committee, so we appreciate it. Thank you very much. Thank you for having us. Okay.

Thank you. Ka kite ano. We'll move on now. Just checking we don't have the Wellington Community Justice Project online, I don't believe. So we'll keep moving forward now. Perhaps we have Swakshadeep Sarkar available for our select committee now.

Just jumped around a little bit, so forgive us. Hopefully, those that are waiting online are available. There we go. Welcome. And my apologies if I've mispronounced your name. We have five minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions. Kia ora. Thank you so much for having me.

I'm Swakshadeep, and I finished my PhD last year at Vic, where I worked with trans asylum seekers and refugees in New Zealand. So I'm here to strongly oppose the Immigration Enhanced Risk Management Amendment Bill. Over the course of oral submissions, a lot of technical gaps in the bill have already been covered.

And since I have very little time, so I'll only focus on one of the human aspects of the bill. This doesn't mean that this is the only aspect that needs to be highlighted, but one of the many. So, I'm going to specifically talk about the amendments to the Section 280 of the bill, which introduces increased surveillance by immigration officers within the community and at workplaces. So while this is unwelcoming for all migrant communities, especially those of color due to the racialization of migrants, which there's a long history of,

it will disproportionately impact asylum seekers and refugees. This amendment, beyond just being a minor update, as the Honorable Minister was saying in an earlier session, particularly harms their process of homemaking and integration. In one of the earlier sessions, I also heard that anyone behaving suspiciously will be stopped by the immigration officials and questioned. I wanted to ask what exactly is suspicious, or as in the bill, what is good cause to believe that migrants may be violating visa conditions or may be here

illegally? This is a highly subjective threshold. Many asylum seekers are scared of enforcement authorities because of their experiences in their home countries as well as here in New Zealand. Not too long ago, it was announced that New Zealand would stop imprisoning people who are seeking asylum in New Zealand.

This distress of imprisonment that many asylum seekers have faced in New Zealand lives in the communities. There are so many testimonies-of asylum seekers being imprisoned during the process of claiming asylum, and they are still undergoing mental health challenges because of their experiences during imprisonment.

So if someone feels anxious at the sight of an immigration officer, would that be deemed as suspicious? An overarching question that needs to be asked is would anyone feel safe at a place where their identities and behavior are always under scrutiny? Research has shown that in New Zealand, asylum seekers and refugees already face a lot of scrutiny when accessing different institutions and services. This amendment adds to the scrutiny, resulting in a feeling of non-belongingness.

Promoting integration for asylum seekers and refugees is a moral, ethical and legal responsibility of New Zealand under the international conventions, and this bill does exactly the opposite. The wider logic of this bill and the messaging, unsupported by data, as many submissions have already mentioned, is to create an atmosphere of fear amongst the migrant and refugee communities.

I also want to conclude by saying that effective immigration policy is about maintaining trust between migrant communities and the state. When surveillance becomes the dominant framework of engagement, it risks undermining the trust, which is essential for genuine integration and safety for everyone in New Zealand. And with that, I end.

Thank you so much for having me. Thank you very much, Swatchadeep. It doesn't appear that we have any questions from our committee, but that does not reflect a lack of interest. It means you've given us a very helpful-- Oh, okay. Ricardo, we've got time for one very quick question. Yeah. Thank you very much. What's the evidential basis you've seen for the changes? I think the evidential basis that I've seen for these changes are actually unsupported by any data. To me, some of that really appear to be well thought about rather than something emerging out of the engagement with the communities. Yeah. Thank you so much. Thank you very much, Swatchadeep. Appreciate your participation in this process. Thank you. And we'll move on now to our next submitter. We have Te Rangi Cleaver.

Te Rangi, we have five minutes with you this morning. Welcome. Thank you for being part of this process, and we'll give the time to you now. Kia ora. Ko Te Rangi ahau. He uri ahau nō Ngāti Kahungunu ki Wairoa. I am a poet, so my kupu comes from my puku. I am of Māori and Sri Lankan descent.

One part of my whakapapa has belonged to this whenua for hundreds of years, and the other crossed oceans to arrive here. I understand intimately that movement has always been part of the human story. And I cannot help but notice how convenient it is whenever this country faces economic pressure, a vulnerable community suddenly gets pulled from a hat and turned into a target.

My last select committee hearing was postponed to today because this government was busy advancing legislation targeting transgender people instead. Somehow, the ones already carrying the most pain become responsible for everything this government has broken, and today, I suppose, we've landed on migrants. Not landlords or billionaires or corporations making record profits while people struggle, or this government cutting thousands of public sector jobs just last week.

Austerity politicians create the conditions for desperation and then act shocked when people demand someone to blame. And this country already has a history of using state-sanctioned violence against migrant communities to distract from its political failures. The dawn raids were also justified through the language of immigration enforcement, and now we look back on it as a chapter of shame.

So I think this committee should ask itself very carefully what kind of political legacy it wants to leave behind this time. There was no game plan there, and there is no game plan here. This amendment bill acts like it exists independently from the global rise in anti-migrant politics currently spreading across the Western world. I know this country does not want the spectacle of ICE.

It doesn't want the international shame of the Rwanda deportation scheme. So instead, you create your own polished New Zealand version, your own clean, green crackdown. Politics never exists in a vacuum, so to the Pākehā MPs on this committee supporting this amendment, I'll ask you plainly, how do you think your tupuna got here?

For real. Did the Crown fill out the correct paperwork? Did it pass character assessments? Did it prove it was economically useful before occupying Māori land? Settler colonialism was never subjected to this kind of scrutiny, and there is such an irony in listening to descendants of settlers construct a moral panic around migration now. Especially when many of the people targeted by increasingly punitive border regimes come from nations shaped by colonial extraction carried out by the very same global powers now criminalizing their movement.

The West sets fire to entire regions and then criminalizes the people fleeing the smoke. And honestly, I find it especially pathetic watching MPs from immigrant backgrounds support legislation like this. Pretending political proximity to power erases the histories that shaped their own communities, because once you make it through the door, they teach you to pull the ladder up behind you, don't they?

You do not get to speak proudly about your migrant story while supporting policies that force other migrant communities to live in deeper fear, because even when these communities get here and they do everything right and they check all the right boxes, you still want to be able to hold the blade above their necks. Prove your humanity over and over and over again. Be monitored, be investigated, be questioned on suspicion, be turned into paperwork, spend your life proving you deserve to exist here. Be silent, be perfect, be

grateful, or we will send you back.What this bill makes clear is who this government considers inherently disposable, because I'm guessing Peter Thiel doesn't get stopped in the street and questioned on suspicion. Funny how that works. New Zealand celebrates its migrant success stories while constructing suspicion around migrants who arrive without wealth, without whiteness, or geopolitical usefulness.

We see the political strategy. We know why migrants are being targeted. We are not naive enough to mistake your scapegoating for solutions. Migrants are our whānau. They are exhausted parents, children trying to feel safe, people building lives while politicians debate whether they deserve dignity at all.

I implore this committee to reject this bill in full, and I will not be taking any questions. Thank you. Thank you, Te Rangi. I appreciate your involvement in the process. And we'll move on now to Kaye Jones. Kaye?

Hopefully, we have you ready. Let's check if-- Oh, we do. Wonderful. Kaye, welcome. There we go. Kia ora, committee. Kia ora. We have five minutes with you this morning. That includes time for questions. We'll pass it over to you now. My name is Kaye Jones. I'm a former policy advisor and more recently a community volunteer.

One of my areas of community activism is pushing for laws to be fair, just and humane. In my view, this bill falls short of that standard. I'm not an immigration lawyer, nor a professional advisor to migrants and refugees seeking to live and work or study or settle in Aotearoa. I have, however, met people in these situations and have heard their stories on why they're here and what their hopes are.

I won't read my submission, but I will highlight one point. Some people are particularly vulnerable to accusations of falsifying documents or biometric information. These include gender minority people, whether they're transgender or non-binary, or have an innate variation of sexual characteristics known as intersex.

If the bill requires matching biometric documents to avoid accusations of fraud that could inadvertently target for exclusion trans and non-binary migrants who arrive in Aotearoa, that they arrive with documents that may reflect a birth label, but not who they really are.

A trans woman may travel as a man because that's the only way she can leave another country. On arriving at a safer country, she may feel the freedom to transform her appearance and her gender expression even before or without getting documents to match her identity. Or she may have documents that show that, but there are also documents that do not match.

In my written submission, I mention Eliana Rubashkin. She is one of many gender minority people whose original travel documents don't match who they are now. They're not lying or trying to commit fraud. They're trying to live as their real selves. Sometimes a change in appearance reflects their ability to access gender affirmation treatment in Aotearoa that wasn't safe before.

Eliana's talked of gaining freedom being here in Aotearoa. That feeling of safety is something she can't describe. The feeling of, "I'm walking in the street and I'm not going to die.
